title: Scofield Reference Bible

infobox.value: is a widely circulated [study Bible](/wiki/Study_Bible "Study Bible"). Edited and annotated by the American Bible student [Cyrus I. Scofield](/wiki/Cyrus_I._Scofield "Cyrus I. Scofield"), it popularized [dispensationalism](/wiki/Dispensationalism "Dispensationalism") at the beginning of the 20th century. Published by [Oxford University Press](/wiki/Oxford_University_Press "Oxford University Press") and containing the entire text of the traditional, [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") [King James Version](/wiki/King_James_Version "King James Version"), it first appeared in 1909 and was revised by the author in 1917.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

The title page listed seven "consulting editors": Henry G. Weston, [James M. Gray](/wiki/James_M._Gray "James M. Gray"), [W.J. Erdman](/w/index.php?title=W.J._Erdman&action=edit&redlink=1 "W.J. Erdman (page does not exist)"), [A.T. Pierson](/wiki/Arthur_Tappan_Pierson "Arthur Tappan Pierson"), W. G. Moorehead, [Elmore Harris](/wiki/Elmore_Harris "Elmore Harris"), and [A. C. Gaebelein](/wiki/Arno_Clemens_Gaebelein "Arno Clemens Gaebelein"). "Just what role these consulting editors played in the project has been the subject of some debate. Apparently Scofield only meant to acknowledge their assistance, though some have speculated that he hoped to gain support for his publication from both sides of the millenarian movement with this device." Ernest Sandeen, *The Roots of Fundamentalism: British and American Millenarianism, 1800-1930* (Chicago: University of Chicago Press, 1970), 224.

Gordon Campbell, *Bible: The Story of the King James Version, 1611-2011* (Oxford University Press, 2010), 26. The Scofield Bible was the predecessor of the "very successful marketing trend" of orienting Bible study tools to average laymen. Mangum & Sweetnam, 172.

Ussher's dates and the gap theory are "not completely congruous with one another," Ussher's dates implying a [young earth](/wiki/Young_Earth_creationism "Young Earth creationism"), and the "gap" between the first two verses of Genesis—as well as Scofield's allowance of the [day-age](/wiki/Day-Age_creationism "Day-Age creationism") theory—suggesting the possibility of an [old earth](/wiki/Old_Earth_creationism "Old Earth creationism"). Mangum & Sweetnam, 97.
Mangum & Sweetnam, 201. The text of King James Version remains under [Crown Copyright](/wiki/Crown_Copyright "Crown Copyright").

Mangum & Sweetnam, 201. "The continuing popularity of the 1917 notes may reflect the preference of the purchasers for the original and *full-strength* Scofield." Mangum & Sweetnam suggest the popularity of the 1917 edition may also reflect a strong commitment to the KJV translation. Scofield was accused of promoting "two ways of salvation" with a dispensation of works before the death and resurrection of Christ and a dispensation of grace afterwards. In the revision of 1967, Scofield's note on John 1:17 "was rewritten, and now seemed to say the opposite of Scofield's original." Gordon Campbell, *Bible: The Story of the King James Version, 1611-2011* (Oxford University Press, 2010), 246-47.

[*The Scofield Study Bible III, KJV: How to use this study Bible*](https://books.google.com/books?id=FCPBVjNnF2oC&pg=PR11). Oxford University Press. 2003. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [9780199723874](/wiki/Special:BookSources/9780199723874 "Special:BookSources/9780199723874"). Retrieved 10 December 2015.
Mangum & Sweetnam, 202-03. Some of the notes have also appeared in Korean and Polynesian.
Bruce Bawer, *Stealing Jesus: How Fundamentalism Betrays Christianity* (New York: Three Rivers Press, 1997).

Magnum & Sweetnam, 188-195. "Historically speaking, The Scofield Reference Bible was to dispensationalism what [Luther](/wiki/Martin_Luther "Martin Luther")'s *[Ninety-five Theses](/wiki/Ninety-five_Theses "Ninety-five Theses")* was to [Lutheranism](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism"), or what [Calvin](/wiki/John_Calvin "John Calvin")'s *[Institutes](/wiki/Institutes_of_the_Christian_Religion "Institutes of the Christian Religion")* was to [Calvinism](/wiki/Calvinism "Calvinism")." (195).

And the Spirit of God moved upon the face of the waters. And God said, Let there be light: and there was light.  [John 3:16](/wiki/John_3:16 "John 3:16")  For God so loved the world, that he gave his only begotten Son, that whosoever believeth in him should not perish, but have everlasting life. | |

The **Scofield Reference Bible** is a widely circulated [study Bible](/wiki/Study_Bible "Study Bible"). Edited and annotated by the American Bible student [Cyrus I. Scofield](/wiki/Cyrus_I._Scofield "Cyrus I. Scofield"), it popularized [dispensationalism](/wiki/Dispensationalism "Dispensationalism") at the beginning of the 20th century. Published by [Oxford University Press](/wiki/Oxford_University_Press "Oxford University Press") and containing the entire text of the traditional, [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") [King James Version](/wiki/King_James_Version "King James Version"), it first appeared in 1909 and was revised by the author in 1917.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

## Features

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scofield_Reference_Bible&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Features")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7f/ScofieldBible.jpg/250px-ScofieldBible.jpg)](/wiki/File:ScofieldBible.jpg)

Scofield Reference Bible, page 1115. This page includes Scofield's note on John 1:17.

The Scofield Bible had several innovative features. Most important, it printed what amounted to a [commentary](/wiki/Exegesis "Exegesis") on the biblical text alongside the Bible instead of in a separate volume, the first to do so in English since the [Geneva Bible](/wiki/Geneva_Bible "Geneva Bible") (1560).[[2]](#cite_note-2) It also contained a [cross-referencing](/wiki/Cross-reference "Cross-reference") system that tied together related verses of Scripture and allowed a reader to follow biblical themes from one chapter and book to another (so called "chain references"). Finally, the 1917 edition also attempted to date events of the Bible. It was in the pages of the Scofield Reference Bible that many Christians first encountered Archbishop [James Ussher](/wiki/James_Ussher "James Ussher")'s calculation of the [date of Creation](/wiki/Ussher_chronology "Ussher chronology") as 4004 BC; and through discussion of Scofield's notes, which advocated the "[gap theory](/wiki/Gap_Creationism "Gap Creationism")," [fundamentalists](/wiki/Christian_fundamentalism "Christian fundamentalism") began a serious internal debate about the nature and chronology of [creation](/wiki/Genesis_creation_myth "Genesis creation myth").[[3]](#cite_note-3)

### Later editions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scofield_Reference_Bible&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Later editions")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/Scofield_Bible_Notes.jpg/250px-Scofield_Bible_Notes.jpg)](/wiki/File:Scofield_Bible_Notes.jpg)

Notes in the 1917 Scofield Bible

The 1917 Scofield Reference Bible notes are now in the [public domain](/wiki/Public_domain "Public domain"), and the 1917 edition is "consistently the best selling edition of the Scofield Bible" in the United Kingdom and Ireland.[[4]](#cite_note-4) In 1967, Oxford University Press published a revision of the Scofield Bible with a slightly modernized KJV text, and a muting of some of the tenets of Scofield's theology.[[5]](#cite_note-5) Recent editions of the KJV Scofield Study Bible have moved the textual changes made in 1967 to the margin.[[6]](#cite_note-kjbchange-6) The Press continues to issue editions under the title *Oxford Scofield Study Bible*, and there are translations into French, German, Spanish, and Portuguese. For instance, the French edition published by the Geneva Bible Society is printed with a revised version of the [Louis Segond](/wiki/Louis_Segond "Louis Segond") translation that includes additional notes by a Francophone committee.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

In the 21st century, Oxford University Press published Scofield notes to accompany six additional English translations.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

## Criticism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scofield_Reference_Bible&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Criticism")]

Opponents of biblical fundamentalism have criticized the Scofield Bible for its air of total authority in biblical interpretation, for what they consider its glossing over of biblical contradictions, and for its focus on eschatology.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

## Legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scofield_Reference_Bible&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Legacy")]

The first edition of the Scofield Bible (1909) was published only a few years before [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), a war that destroyed a cultural optimism that had viewed the world as entering a new era of peace and prosperity; then the post-[World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") era witnessed the creation of a homeland for [Jews](/wiki/Jews "Jews") in [Palestine](/wiki/Mandatory_Palestine "Mandatory Palestine"). Thus, Scofield's [premillennialism](/wiki/Premillennialism "Premillennialism") seemed prophetic. "At the popular level, especially, many people came to regard the dispensationalist scheme as completely vindicated."[[10]](#cite_note-10) Sales of the Reference Bible exceeded two million copies by the end of World War II.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

The Scofield Reference Bible promoted [dispensationalism](/wiki/Dispensationalism "Dispensationalism"), the belief that between creation and the [final judgement](/wiki/Last_Judgment "Last Judgment") there would be seven distinct eras of God's dealing with man and that these eras are a framework for synthesizing the message of the Bible.[[12]](#cite_note-12) Largely through the influence of Scofield's notes, many [fundamentalist Christians](/wiki/Christian_fundamentalism "Christian fundamentalism") in the United States adopted a dispensational theology. Scofield's notes on the [Book of Revelation](/wiki/Book_of_Revelation "Book of Revelation") are a major source for the various timetables, judgements, and plagues elaborated on by popular religious writers such as [Hal Lindsey](/wiki/Hal_Lindsey "Hal Lindsey"), [Edgar C. Whisenant](/wiki/Edgar_C._Whisenant "Edgar C. Whisenant"), and [Tim LaHaye](/wiki/Tim_LaHaye "Tim LaHaye");[[13]](#cite_note-13) and in part because of the success of the Scofield Reference Bible, twentieth-century American fundamentalists placed greater stress on [eschatological](/wiki/Eschatology "Eschatology") speculation.

The *Scofield Bible* significantly influenced the [Christian Zionist](/wiki/Christian_Zionism "Christian Zionism") movement. Referring to Scofield's interpretation of Genesis 12:3 ("I will bless them that bless thee and curse him that curseth thee"), [John Hagee](/wiki/John_Hagee "John Hagee"), the founder of [Christians United for Israel](/wiki/Christians_United_for_Israel "Christians United for Israel") (CUFI), argued that "The man or nation that lifts a voice or hand against Israel invites the wrath of God."[[14]](#cite_note-14)

## Further reading

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scofield_Reference_Bible&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Further reading")]

* [Arno C. Gaebelein](/wiki/Arno_Clemens_Gaebelein "Arno Clemens Gaebelein"), *The History of the Scofield Reference Bible* (Our Hope Publications, 1943)
* William E. Cox, *Why I Left Scofieldism* (Phillipsburg, N.J.: Presbyterian and Reformed Publishing Co., 1975) [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [0-87552-154-1](/wiki/Special:BookSources/0-87552-154-1 "Special:BookSources/0-87552-154-1")
* R. Todd Mangum and Mark S. Sweetnam, *The Scofield Bible: Its History and Impact on the Evangelical Church* (Colorado Springs: Paternoster Publishing, 2009)
* Donald Harman Akenson, *The Americanization of the Apocalypse: Creating America's Own Bible* (New York: Oxford University Press, 2023)
